title: TEC Prediction with GNSS–Aditya-L1 & LSTM
desc: Time-series prediction of ionospheric TEC using GNSS and Aditya-L1 solar data.
content:
Overview

This project develops a data-driven framework to predict ionospheric Total Electron Content (TEC) by integrating GNSS observations with solar activity data from Aditya-L1. The work focuses on understanding the nonlinear coupling between solar dynamics and ionospheric behaviour using both statistical analysis and deep learning.

Problem

Ionospheric TEC variations significantly affect GNSS signal accuracy, causing delays and positioning errors in navigation systems. Traditional models lack the ability to capture short-term fluctuations driven by solar events such as flares and solar wind disturbances, making real-time prediction challenging.

Data & Preprocessing

  • GNSS data from AAI stations (Ahmedabad, Trivandrum, Shimla)
  • STEC computed from dual-frequency measurements and converted to VTEC using mapping functions
  • Elevation filtering (>30°) and median aggregation across satellites for noise reduction
  • Solar data from Aditya-L1:
    • SOLEXS: X-ray photon flux (2–22 keV)
    • ASPEX: Ion flux (TH1, TH2) and plasma parameters
  • Time synchronization across datasets with ±5s tolerance
  • Interpolation and cleaning for continuous time-series generation

Methodology

  • Correlation analysis using Spearman and Mutual Information
  • Feature engineering with temporal inputs
  • 24-timestep sequence modeling
  • LSTM (32 units) + Dropout + Dense layers

Results

  • Ahmedabad: RMSE ≈ 3.61 TECU, MAPE ≈ 7.25%
  • Shimla: RMSE ≈ 2.77 TECU, MAPE ≈ 6.68%
  • Trivandrum: RMSE ≈ 1.91 TECU, MAPE ≈ 3.69%
  • Robust under 10–50% noise
  • Cross-location generalization achieved (MAPE ≈ 15%)

Key Contributions

  • End-to-end TEC prediction pipeline
  • Fusion of GNSS and Aditya-L1 solar data
  • Deep learning-based ionospheric modeling
  • Robustness and generalization validation

Tech Stack

Python • TensorFlow/Keras • Pandas • NumPy • CDFlib • Astropy

title: Extended Kalman Filter for GPS Positioning
desc: State estimation and noise-aware GPS positioning using EKF.
content:
Overview

This project develops an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) framework for GPS-based positioning by modeling nonlinear satellite–receiver dynamics and measurement uncertainties. The work focuses on recursive state estimation under varying motion conditions, including stationary, low-dynamic, and high-dynamic scenarios.

Problem

GPS positioning relies on nonlinear pseudorange measurements affected by multiple sources of error such as atmospheric delay, clock bias, multipath effects, and measurement noise. Classical linear methods fail to accurately estimate receiver position and velocity in dynamic environments, requiring a robust nonlinear estimation approach.

Modeling

  • Nonlinear pseudorange equations based on satellite–receiver geometry
  • Linearization using Jacobian matrices
  • State vector including position, velocity, clock bias, and clock drift
  • Dynamic models for stationary, low-dynamic, and high-dynamic systems

Methodology

  • Initialization using least squares / Gauss–Newton method
  • State prediction using system dynamics (F matrix)
  • Measurement update using observation model (H matrix)
  • Covariance propagation and Kalman gain computation
  • Recursive prediction–update cycle

Noise & Uncertainty

  • Process noise modeling using covariance matrices
  • Measurement noise from atmospheric and receiver errors
  • Clock bias modeled as stochastic process
  • Allan variance for realistic clock behavior

Results

  • Accurate position and velocity estimation
  • Stable convergence under noisy conditions
  • Robust performance across dynamic scenarios
  • Effective handling of nonlinear GPS measurements

Tech Stack

Python • Kalman Filtering • State Estimation • Linear Algebra • Numerical Methods
